items.title:
@andykonwinski Exactly. I've been disseminating a similar message for years. 

The concentration of ...
items.content:
@andykonwinski Exactly. I've been disseminating a similar message for years. 

The concentration of power in AI and the desire for control is by far the biggest danger of AI. It could lead to a few private companies and/or countries being in control of access to information, access to knowledge, and access to the tools of economic expansion. 
It's a kind of medieval obscurantism akin to the Ottoman empire banning the use of the printing press for 200 years, in part to keep control of the dogma, but also to protect the corporation of the calligraphers and scribes.

Relevant historical bits about the Internet:
1. It took a deliberate decision by Al Gore and Bill Clinton to open up access of what was then ARPAnet to commercial entities and to the public, against the desires of the entrenched telecom industry. During a public roundtable about the "information superhighway" in 1993, the CEO of AT&T told Gore and Clinton "leave it to us". Gore said no.
2. In the late 1980s, setting up an Internet presence required buying proprietary hardware with proprietary OS and software stack from Sun Microsystems, HP, IBM, or Dell. By the 2000s, all of this was wiped out by commodity hardware, Linux, Apache, and an entirely free/open software stack. This migration to open platforms was the result of market forces.

Infrastructure wants to be open.
Foundation models are becoming an infrastructure and will inevitably become commoditized.
Long term, the money is in the application layer, which is what I, Arthur Mensch, Alex Karp, and others have been saying.

items.title:
One of the clearest arguments I've read for why openness matters.

Worth 2 minutes of your time. @an...
items.content:
One of the clearest arguments I've read for why openness matters.

Worth 2 minutes of your time. @andykonwinski puts into words something many of us have been feeling:

--

"Democracy is built on a profound skepticism of concentrated power. Open science shares this principle. Both are built on the idea that progress and legitimacy emerge from broad, distributed participation rather than concentrated, gated authority."

"If our best scientists and engineers can only reach the frontier by joining a handful of secretive labs, we do not have an open research ecosystem. We do not have a truly competitive market. We have a system in which participation increasingly depends on the permission of a few individuals at a small number of private companies."

"The challenge now is to build a new commons at the intersection of academia, industry, and the public interest.

This research commons must be ambitious enough to matter. It will require frontier-scale compute, access to state-of-the-art models, operational support, public investment, and philanthropic capital. It will require companies willing to contribute to an ecosystem larger than themselves, so that they can continue to benefit from open research."

items.title: 🎙️ @Alibaba_Qwen's Qwen3-Omni listens, reasons, and talks back. Serving that in real time is a pipel...
items.content:
🎙️ @Alibaba_Qwen's Qwen3-Omni listens, reasons, and talks back. Serving that in real time is a pipeline problem, not a single model: a multimodal Thinker, then Talker → Code2Wav for the speech.

Each stage bottlenecks differently, so the wins come from optimizing them layer by layer. One neat trick: under load, replicate only the two speech stages and let the heavy multimodal Thinker run once. At high concurrency that lands first audio in ~0.6s instead of ~6s, speech faster than real time, and ~5.4x the throughput on the same GPUs.

Built with @AntGroup's Super Computing Technology (SCT) team and the vLLM-Omni team. The blog breaks down the full stack, one bottleneck at a time 👇
🔗 https://t.co/aKWZdhNzMU

items.title:
Here is how to build your first agentic loop with Claude Code:

1. Open your terminal.
2. Create an ...
items.content:
Here is how to build your first agentic loop with Claude Code:

1. Open your terminal.
2. Create an empty folder and move into it.
3. Run the following command:

*************************
claude -p "Write fibonnacy(n) in a Python file. Write tests for it, including edge cases. Run pytest after every change. Don't stop until every test passes." \
  --allowedTools "Read,Write,Edit,Bash(python3 -m pytest:*),Bash(pytest:*)"\
  --max-turns 15
*************************

That's it. If you have Python and pytest installed on your system, Claude will implement the Fibonacci function and test it.

There are 3 things you need to know about this command:

First, it uses the -p flag to run Claude Code non-interactively. It takes a prompt, runs the full agent loop, prints the result, and exits.

Second, the --allowedTools flag pre-approves exactly what the agent can touch, so Claude doesn't need to ask you every time.

Third, the --max-turns flag caps the number of agentic turns the loop can run. When it hits the cap, the run exits with an error instead of grinding forever.

This is just a simple "Hello World"-type example to get you started.

The key on every loop is the verification step: how does the agent know it is done?

Coming up with the evaluation criteria is probably the most important step in building a loop. In this example, I'm asking Claude to write unit tests and use them to evaluate the code. This works for something simple, but you'll need a much more robust evaluation for anything more complex.

Something worth mentioning: building the verification step is where a ton of my time is going right now. The agent is writing the code, and the agent is verifying that the code (and the product) work as intended. My job has become defining what "works" looks like.

items.title:
Evaluating Large Language Models in Scientific Discovery: https://t.co/nKnJVA5nmE

Excellent review ...
items.content:
Evaluating Large Language Models in Scientific Discovery: https://t.co/nKnJVA5nmE

Excellent review of this research paper below, from @alex_prompter 

To me, the most significant summary statement is this: “LLMs optimize for plausibility, not truth.”

That’s so perfectly Bayesian! I love Bayesian, but not in this circumstance. Important scientific discoveries are often in the noise, not the signal; in the anomaly, not the dominant pattern; in the unexpected, not the historical precedents; in viewing with a different perspective (e.g., in a new set of data dimensions), not in the one prescribed in the published literature; in exploring an inspired new hypothesis, not in optimizing and finding the “winner” among the set of known hypotheses.

This is not for people who build World Models.

I get an exclusive look inside one of the best AI labs in the world in Silicon Valley, Institute of Foundation Models, and talk with Hector Liu, @waterluffy, who leads World Model research there.

These will be the “brains” of humanoid robots, and the key tech for all sorts of other things.

I met with him to try to figure out how far away a truly generalized robot is.

More on this lab and what they do at: https://t.co/fY4qGOB9WJ

items.title: When brainstorming new AI agent uses, I always remind myself what used to be expensive and is now ne...
items.content:
When brainstorming new AI agent uses, I always remind myself what used to be expensive and is now nearly zero…

1) cost of reading everything fell → you can now watch 100% instead of a sample (every expense, every call, every camera)

2) cost of making one more fell → abundance (12 custom onboarding decks instead of one, 100 social posts generated from one book)

3) cost of an expert opinion fell → a reviewer on every doc (every contract I sign goes through one in addition to my lawyer, medical results, scientific paper explanations)

4) cost of a conversation fell → one tutor/coach/SDR per person

5) cost of trying fell → ginkgo bioworks running 36000 experiments, Sherpa trying thousands of websites 

6) cost of whole new roles fell → I hired a “dreamer” (Phoebe) who overlays my business and ideates and tests new ways of thinking/working , hired Toby who maintains memory and efficiency across my org - two roles I wouldn’t have hired a dedicated human for
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie Miller lists six categories where AI agents drive costs toward zero, including reading everything, mass generation, expert review, tutoring, experimentation, and enabling entirely new roles she has hired for.
items.reasoning: Original, framework-style thinking from a well-known AI voice with strong engagement and concrete examples, offering genuine practical insight into agentic AI applications.

When brainstorming new AI agent uses, I always remind myself what used to be expensive and is now nearly zero…

3) cost of an expert opinion fell → a reviewer on every doc (every contract I sign goes through one in addition to my lawyer, medical results, scientific paper explanations)

As I mentioned in the post, I don't necessarily like this for anything more complex than a simple demo. 

Unit tests alone are not enough to make a great validator (ideally, you have several other ways to evaluate what the agent did).

/6 Harvard just open-sourced a full curriculum for Machine Learning Systems.

It teaches AI engineer...
items.content:
/6 Harvard just open-sourced a full curriculum for Machine Learning Systems.

It teaches AI engineering beyond model training, covering distributed training, production inference, edge deployment, hardware limits, fault tolerance, and governance.

> Two-volume textbook
> TinyTorch for building a deep learning framework
> Hardware kits for Arduino and Raspberry Pi

@daniel_mac8 @elonmusk @teortaxesTex Bell Labs was the R&D arm of AT&T. 
AT&T was a company (a regulated monopoly until 1984).
Most of Bell Labs (25000 people in the late 1980s) was doing D. 
Only a small portion, called Area 11 (about 1200 people) was doing R and publishing its research.
Almost all the discoveries and innovations from Bell Labs you heard about originally came from Area 11: transistor, solar cells, cosmic background radiation, CCD, Unix, C/C++, convolutional nets, and countless others.

items.title:
Bigger context windows won't fix your agent's memory problem.

Memory isn't a longer prompt. It's a ...
items.content:
Bigger context windows won't fix your agent's memory problem.

Memory isn't a longer prompt. It's a read-write discipline: what to save, recall, and forget.

Most agents have four kinds, and skip most of them:

> working: what's on the desk
> episodic: what it already tried
> semantic: facts about your data
> procedural: skills it can reuse

Retrieval is the other half. Your model has never seen your data.

It has to fetch the right piece at the right moment. It gets sharper when the agent runs the search itself.

Look, read, notice a gap, search again. The fix is a vector store and a write policy, not more tokens.

Skip both and your agent guesses, then re-solves the same problem tomorrow. Get them right and it stops repeating itself, and citing the wrong file.

The article below, It walks the full stack: 
> retrieval and memory systems 
> the harness and orchestration 
> tools, MCP, and A2A 
> multi-agent coordination and evaluation

items.title: You may think your security team can respond in time, but attackers are already using automation and...
items.content:
You may think your security team can respond in time, but attackers are already using automation and AI to move faster.

Defensive AI agents can help close that speed gap while human oversight keeps every automated action under clear control.

Microblog  by @antgrasso  #AiAgents

The point is not to remove people from cybersecurity.

It is to protect their attention and give them more time for the decisions that require judgment.

AI agents can monitor systems continuously, detect anomalies earlier, and trigger controlled first responses before damage expands.

Human teams should focus on the choices that carry real consequences: containment, escalation, legal exposure, business continuity, and trust.

In cybersecurity, machines can handle speed, but people must keep responsibility and final control.

/5 Supervision has become one of the core open-source toolkits for computer vision builders.

It is a model-agnostic Python library from Roboflow that helps you build CV apps around classification, detection, and segmentation models.

> 45k GitHub stars

> 5,000 commits and 1,500+ pull requests

> 150+ contributors

> Powers 6.5k+ open-source CV projects

> Supports annotation, tracking, zone counting, datasets, and benchmarking

Computer vision apps need more than models.

Repo: https://t.co/jztqTXoANV

It lets agents read and search platforms like Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, and XiaoHongShu from one CLI.

> Local cookies for privacy
> Primary and fallback backends

> Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, and other agents

Agents need more than Google results.

items.title: While I was working on chapters 4-6 (whose drafts are ready by the way), many folks read and comment...
items.content:
While I was working on chapters 4-6 (whose drafts are ready by the way), many folks read and commented on the drafts of the first three chapters, and I should tell you: WHAT A PLEASURE to read reviews from humans! The comments, corrections, and suggestions DO MAKE SENSE!

It's not a dumb pattern matching or "let me just generate something because I wasn't finetuned to shut up."

Real pleasure reading these comments from real people, I must say. The text visibly becomes a step closer to perfection when I implement the proposals.

(If you would like to contribute, it's not too late. Please DM me if you want to volunteer.)

items.title:
Do you have wide knowledge or deep knowledge?

My knowledge is very wide. I am not just talking abou...
items.content:
Do you have wide knowledge or deep knowledge?

My knowledge is very wide. I am not just talking about my experiences, such as:
1. Visiting San Quentin Prison
2. Being on the deck of an aircraft carrier
3. Getting a tour of Israel with the Israeli ambassador
4. Being on a private jet when a candidate announces he is running for President of the United States

Yes, I have all that experience, but my knowledge of the tech industry is also very wide, but it is not very deep. My friend Adrian Kaehler can write math equations on a whiteboard for how an autonomous car goes through an intersection; that is not me (he built the computer vision system for the first Waymo). 

You can see this difference in this video here. I know more about Tesla FSD than Hector does.

But I guarantee you, his knowledge about AI is far deeper than mine. It makes for an interesting conversation. Tried to ask intelligent questions.

Most entrepreneurs tend to be deep on their topic or the market they are trying to serve. But often they don’t know much about things in other domains.

But every once in a while, somebody comes along who is both a wide and a deep thinker. Out of the group of all of us, they tend to be the special humans who stand out.

I am blessed to be following 50,000 thinkers. Thank you for making my life so interesting trying to keep up.

My freeway thinking. :-)

items.title: @dennismiloseski I saw a drone company once who did this with Kickstarter. They got $11 million in o...
items.content:
@dennismiloseski I saw a drone company once who did this with Kickstarter. They got $11 million in orders and then learned they couldn't make the drone because they didn't sell enough for the Chinese factories to care about them. 

So yeah, it is a very dangerous precedent. Building a factory and making something is a whole other skill than making the first prototype. 

If I was investing in a company that promised hardware, I'd want to see some demonstration that they know how to:
1. Make a factory
2. Make it produce the product

"AI-Native LLM Security — Threats, Defense...
items.content:
From @PacktDataML — available at https://t.co/fmHgkRo26I

"AI-Native LLM Security — Threats, Defenses, and Best Practices for Building Safe and Trustworthy AI"

𝘽𝙤𝙤𝙠 𝘿𝙚𝙨𝙘𝙧𝙞𝙥𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣:
"Adversarial AI attacks present a unique set of security challenges, exploiting the very foundation of how AI learns. This book explores these threats in depth, equipping cybersecurity professionals with the tools needed to secure generative AI and LLM applications. Rather than skimming the surface of emerging risks, it focuses on practical strategies, industry standards, and recent research to build a robust defense framework.

Structured around actionable insights, the chapters introduce a secure-by-design methodology, integrating threat modeling and MLSecOps practices to fortify AI systems. You’ll discover how to leverage established taxonomies from OWASP, NIST, and MITRE to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities. Through real-world examples, the book highlights best practices for incorporating security controls into AI development life cycles, covering key areas such as CI/CD, MLOps, and open-access LLMs."

Happy 250th America! 

Off to have our robot drive us from San Jose to San Diego, will be taking the scenic route. Just installed a new update, so will experience the latest AI. Life is good!

I won't get many more road trips with the boys before they are full adults and out of the house, so special times.
